verses.text: The exiles return province by province, each to his own city, led by Zerubbabel, Jeshua, and the rest. The captives of Babylon come home at last. God keeps His word to the very person and place; the names of His returning people are not lost in the crowd but known to Him.

The exiles return province by province, each to his own city, led by Zerubbabel, Jeshua, and the rest. The captives of Babylon come home at last. God keeps His word to the very person and place; the names of His returning people are not lost in the crowd but known to Him.

The returning families are counted one by one, thousands from each house. God numbers His people as a shepherd counts his flock. No name is too obscure to be written down; the LORD knows each household that answers His call to come home and rebuild.

The priests return in their courses, thousands strong, ready to serve at the altar. The house of God needs its ministers. God brings back not only a people but those set apart to lead them in worship, that His service may begin again.

The priests return in their courses, thousands strong, ready to serve at the altar. The house of God needs its ministers. God brings back not only a people but those set apart to lead them in worship, that His service may begin again.

The priests return in their courses, thousands strong, ready to serve at the altar. The house of God needs its ministers. God brings back not only a people but those set apart to lead them in worship, that His service may begin again.

The priests return in their courses, thousands strong, ready to serve at the altar. The house of God needs its ministers. God brings back not only a people but those set apart to lead them in worship, that His service may begin again.

The Levites, singers, and gatekeepers return, fewer in number than the priests. The humbler offices are filled too. God's house needs its singers and its doorkeepers as well as its priests; every place of service matters in His worship.

The Levites, singers, and gatekeepers return, fewer in number than the priests. The humbler offices are filled too. God's house needs its singers and its doorkeepers as well as its priests; every place of service matters in His worship.

The Nethinim and the children of Solomon's servants — the lowliest temple helpers — are also named and numbered. The menial servants are not forgotten. In God's house the one who carries water is recorded with the rest; faithful service in a small place is honoured by Him.

Some could not prove their Israelite descent, and certain priests, lacking the register, were barred from holy things until a priest with Urim and Thummim should arise. Heritage in God's house must be sure. A claim to serve at the altar had to be genuine, not merely assumed.

The whole assembly numbers over forty-two thousand, besides their servants, singers, and animals. A great company has come home. From a scattered, captive people God gathers a nation again, proving that no exile is beyond the reach of His restoring hand.

Arriving at the house of the LORD, some heads of families give freewill offerings to rebuild it on its old site, then all settle in their cities. They give before they ask. Hearts grateful for restoration open gladly to the work of God's house.
